Party_Details.Name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd (APG) was incorporated on 9 February 2009.
APG was in the business of marketing and selling Australian real estate on behalf of Australian property developers.
APG marketed properties consisting of land and premises with the premises being or about to be constructed.
APG earned revenue from commissions paid by Australian property developers and incurred expenses including rent, staff costs and marketing costs.
APG typically received a Front End Commission upon a buyer signing an unconditional sale contract with a 5% to 10% deposit, and a Back End Commission upon completion of the sale and purchase.
The Front End Commission was typically less than the Back End Commission and was intended to cover APG's operational marketing costs, while the Back End Commission represented mainly profit.
A considerable period of time could elapse between receipt of the Front End Commission and the Back End Commission because completion occurred only after construction of the premises.
On 11 March 2009, APG entered into a loan agreement with Goh Cheng Liang under which APG received a loan of $900,000.
On incorporation, APG issued shares as fully paid-up to several individuals, although the share capital for those shares had not in fact been paid.
Non-executive directors Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren acted to protect the interests of lender Goh Cheng Liang while executive directors controlled APG's day-to-day operations.
APG engaged Firstwaters Consultants Pte Ltd to reconstruct APG's accounts from 2009 and to maintain day-to-day accounting.
APG engaged chartered accountant John Kennedy Stuart to provide tax services and advice to APG's clients and later to investigate and report on APG's financial situation.

On 2 July 2010, John Kennedy Stuart sent an email to APG's directors stating that, based on cash flow difficulties, existing and pending financial commitments and expected debtors, APG was technically insolvent as at that date.

In response to the 2 July 2010 email, Sean Colville Niven wrote that the creditors report and revenue report had been visible to all shareholders for at least six months and that there was nothing new in the summary of APG's cash flow and creditors' position.
In early 2010, APG experienced financial difficulties and was unable to pay rent, advertising agencies and staff salaries, and its accounts were disorganised.
In November 2009, APG borrowed A$900,000 from Ubertas Funds Management Pty Ltd and used the funds to repay the $900,000 loan from Goh Cheng Liang.
The Ubertas loan of A$900,000 was repayable by monthly instalments of A$40,000.
On 6 September 2010, APG, Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene executed a written agreement referred to as the September Agreement.
Under the September Agreement, Ms Lim agreed to capitalise $175,000 owed to her by APG and to inject an additional $150,000 into APG in exchange for 325,000 new shares.
Under the September Agreement, it was recorded that APG owed Ms Lim $175,000 and that Sean Colville Niven owed APG $300,450.37.

The September Agreement provided that APG would write off 40% of the $300,450.37 recorded as owed by Sean Colville Niven, with the remaining $180,270.22 to be paid in two instalments by 31 December 2011 and 31 December 2012.

The September Agreement recorded that unpaid share capital liabilities were allocated as $560,000 to Sean Colville Niven, $160,000 to Wang Zijian and $80,000 to Ms Lim, with 10% of the outstanding capital to be repaid each year for ten years.
On 6 October 2010, by written directors' resolution signed by Sean Colville Niven and Ms Lim, APG approved the appointment of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd to conduct a review of APG's accounts.

On 11 October 2010, Ferrier Hodgson sent APG's board a draft summary of findings from its preliminary review of APG's financial books and records, noting that many payments, including payments to Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, lacked supporting vouchers and that APG had paid personal expenses of Sean Colville Niven.
On 13 October 2010, a handwritten document referred to as the October Agreement was signed by Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Ms Lim, recording terms for the parties to part ways.

The October Agreement recorded that Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would resign as directors on 13 October 2010 and transfer all their shares, except for 10% retained by Wang Zijian, to Ms Lim, and that Ms Lim would pay Sean Colville Niven's outstanding expenses and commission in instalments.

The October Agreement recorded that upon settlement, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would have no liability to APG from a compliance perspective and would owe nothing to APG excluding personal tax liabilities.
In May 2010, APG had difficulty paying its rent and its landlord that APG was more than three months in arrears of rent as at 18 May 2010.
On or about 18 May 2010, Sean Colville Niven emailed APG's landlord asking for understanding and stating that, as with many new businesses, APG had experienced struggles with cash flow.

On 24 June 2010, APG received a demand from its advertising agency to pay an outstanding amount of $59,502.92 by 4 July 2010, with a further communication on 16 July 2010 threatening legal action if payment was not received by 19 July 2010.
In July 2010, APG's advertising agency agreed to accept payment of the outstanding $59,502.92 in two instalments: $20,000 by 20 July 2010 and the balance by 30 July 2010.
In July 2010, Ms Lim injected $25,000 into APG to help fund a marketing event.
In November 2010, Ms Lim repaid the outstanding Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of APG's debt to Ubertas to herself.

After incorporation, APG made various payments to or for the benefit of Sean Colville Niven, including payments of American Express credit card bills, legal fees in relation to Suit No 170 of 2009, payments without supporting documents, travel expenses for him and his family, payments to third parties on his behalf, and a payment of an invoice amount into his personal bank account.

APG's records, as summarised in its pleadings, listed payments to or on behalf of Sean Colville Niven totalling $919,980.97 and A$184,266.68, including American Express expenses of $326,944.34, legal expenses of $48,994.47 and A$1,987.42, payments without proper supporting documents of $264,746.96 and A$90,191.25, travel expenses of $33,328.37, payments to third parties on his behalf of $245,966.83 and A$6,088.43, and a payment of A$86,000 into his personal bank account.

APG paid legal fees to HW Litigation and Butcher Paul & Calder in relation to Suit No 170 of 2009, a suit brought by JL Property Group Pte Ltd against, among others, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, although APG was not a party to that suit.

APG made payments for air travel undertaken by Sean Colville Niven, his wife, his children and the family's domestic helper, including a trip to France by Sean Colville Niven and his wife in August 2009.
APG made payments for telephone charges for Sean Colville Niven and his children, his children's school fees, and rental and leasing of furniture.

In February 2010, APG carried out a marketing campaign for a property developed by Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd and issued an invoice dated 22 February 2010 for A$86,000 for marketing funds, with instructions on the invoice for payment to be made to Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account in Australia.
Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd paid A$86,000 into Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account pursuant to the 22 February 2010 invoice.

APG transferred funds from its bank account into a joint current account held by Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx, during the period from 6 February 2009 to 31 May 2009.

On 22 February 2010, APG raised an invoice to Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd for A$86,000 described as marketing funds for a campaign, signed by Sean Colville Niven with instructions for payment to his personal bank account.

On 6 March 2009, APG's non-executive directors passed a resolution ratifying APG's purchase of a vehicle registered in the name of Sean Colville Niven and requiring him to execute an acknowledgment of trust that he held the vehicle on trust for APG.
APG's articles of association contained provisions (including Articles 17, 19, 20, 32, 34 and 36) governing calls on unpaid share capital and liability of registered shareholders for unpaid amounts.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and financial arrangements, APG's funds were used for payments connected to Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian and whether unpaid share capital recorded for their shareholdings remained outstanding, in circumstances where APG was experiencing cash flow difficulties and had entered into the September and October 2010 agreements reallocating and rescheduling those obligations.
Party_Details.Rule: Company law principles provide that directors and officers are expected to ensure that company funds are applied for company purposes, that payments to directors or related parties should be supported by proper authorisation and documentation, and that shareholders recorded as holding issued shares may be to the company for unpaid share capital, with calls typically enforceable against the registered holder at the time of the call. When a company is insolvent or near insolvency, directors are generally expected to take into account the interests of the company's creditors when entering into transactions that affect the company's asset base.
Party_Details.Application: APG's business model involved deferred Back End Commissions, while it faced immediate obligations such as rent, staff salaries, advertising costs and loan instalments under the Ubertas facility. From early 2010, APG experienced cash flow difficulties, including rental arrears and creditor demands from its advertising agency, and its accountant informed the directors on 2 July 2010 that APG was technically insolvent based on cash flow and commitments. During this period, APG made multiple payments connected to Sean Colville Niven, including credit card bills, legal fees for litigation in which APG was not a party, travel and family-related expenses, and payments without supporting documentation, as well as directing marketing funds from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd to his personal account. APG also transferred funds into a joint account held by Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian. The September Agreement recorded significant amounts as owed by Sean Colville Niven to APG and reallocated unpaid share capital obligations among the shareholders, with a long repayment schedule, while the October Agreement recorded that Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would resign and transfer most of their shares to Ms Lim and that they would not owe liabilities to APG upon settlement except for tax. These arrangements, together with the pattern of payments and the state of APG's finances, framed the later dispute over whether the payments and share capital obligations remained enforceable.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether the defendants, as former directors and shareholders, were liable to repay sums paid out of APG's funds and to pay unpaid share capital. The court characterises the use of company funds for a director's personal use as a prima facie abuse of funds and applies authorities such as Creanovate Pte Ltd v Firstlink Energy Pte Ltd and Ho Kang Peng v Scintronix Corp Ltd. It finds that many payments to or for the benefit of Sean Colville Niven lacked supporting documentation and included clearly personal expenses, and that he did not discharge the burden of justifying them as company expenses. The court rejects the alleged Account Practice and Oral Agreement as unproven and inconsistent with contemporaneous documents, including the loan agreement with Goh Cheng Liang and correspondence from the non-executive directors. It concludes that the September and October Agreements were entered into when APG was insolvent or near insolvency, so the directors owed duties to consider creditors' interests. Applying principles from Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and Liquidators of Progen Engineering Pte Ltd v Progen Holdings Ltd, the court holds that the Agreements, which wrote off or postponed substantial debts owed by the defendants and shifted part of their personal liabilities to APG, were not in the interests of APG's creditors and were voidable. The Agreements are set aside and do not relieve the defendants of liability. On unpaid share capital, the court interprets APG's articles and general company law to hold that calls are enforceable only against the registered holder at the time of the call, so Sean Colville Niven is liable only for unpaid capital on shares he still held when the call was made, while liability for the 240,000 shares transferred to Ms Lim rests with her. The court finds that alleged payments by Sean Colville Niven in November and December 2009 do not establish that his share capital was paid up, noting evidential gaps and that he acknowledged unpaid capital in the September Agreement. The court orders Sean Colville Niven to pay $400,000 for unpaid share capital and to repay $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10 (less amounts recovered from the first defendant) as moneys paid for his personal use. It orders Wang Zijian to pay $160,000 for unpaid share capital. Both defendants are ordered jointly and severally to account for all sums paid by APG into their joint UOB account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes. The court dismisses the defendants' counterclaims, which were premised on the validity of the September Agreement, and notes that any alleged misconduct by Ms Lim does not affect APG's right to recover from the defendants.

Party_Details.Name: Sean Colville Niven

Facts.Text: Sean Colville Niven has been involved in the business of marketing and selling Australian properties since 1989 and has experience and connections with Australian property developers.
Before December 2008, Sean Colville Niven worked with Wang Zijian in JL Property Group Pte Ltd, a company selling Australian properties.
In December 2008, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian decided to start their own company to sell Australian properties and resigned from JL Property Group Pte Ltd.
In December 2008, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian approached Goh Cheng Liang, the godfather of Wang Zijian, for a loan of $900,000 as start-up capital for their new venture.
As a condition of providing the $900,000 loan, Goh Cheng Liang required that his nominees become directors and shareholders of APG.
On incorporation of APG, Sean Colville Niven was a director and shareholder holding 360,000 ordinary shares, which were issued as fully paid-up although the share capital was not actually paid.
After the resignation of the non-executive directors on 7 November 2009, their shares in APG were transferred to Sean Colville Niven, increasing his shareholding to 640,000 shares.
On 15 May 2009, after Ms Lim joined APG, Sean Colville Niven was responsible for securing rights to market properties from Australian developers and managing APG's finances.
On 8 December 2009, Ms Lim was appointed as a director of APG, and in January 2010 she purchased 240,000 shares from Sean Colville Niven for $160,000.
The share transfer form for the January 2010 transfer of 240,000 shares from Sean Colville Niven to Ms Lim stated that the shares were fully paid up.

On 6 September 2010, Sean Colville Niven signed the September Agreement with APG, Wang Zijian and Ms Lim, acknowledging that he owed APG $300,450.37 and agreeing to a partial write-off and instalment repayment of the balance.
Under the September Agreement, it was recorded that Sean Colville Niven was for $560,000 of unpaid share capital and that he would repay 10% of the outstanding capital each year for ten years.
On 6 October 2010, Sean Colville Niven signed a directors' resolution with Ms Lim approving the appointment of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd to review APG's accounts.

Ferrier Hodgson's draft summary of findings dated 11 October 2010 noted that APG had made payments to Sean Colville Niven without supporting vouchers, that APG had paid his personal expenses, and that payments had been made to him in Australia by internet withdrawals and to an Australian company named Australian Property Group (Vic) Pty Ltd without supporting documents.

On 13 October 2010, Sean Colville Niven signed the handwritten October Agreement with Wang Zijian and Ms Lim, which recorded that he would resign as a director that day and transfer all his shares in APG to Ms Lim, and that Ms Lim would pay his outstanding expenses and commission in instalments.
The October Agreement recorded that upon settlement, Sean Colville Niven would have no liability to APG from a compliance perspective and would owe nothing to APG except for personal tax liabilities.

APG's records, as pleaded, listed payments to or on behalf of Sean Colville Niven including American Express credit card expenses of $326,944.34, legal expenses of $48,994.47 and A$1,987.42, payments without proper supporting documents of $264,746.96 and A$90,191.25, travel expenses of $33,328.37, payments to third parties on his behalf of $245,966.83 and A$6,088.43, and a payment of A$86,000 into his personal bank account.

APG paid American Express credit card bills issued to Sean Colville Niven in full each month, including charges for petrol, department stores, supermarkets, restaurants and a charge of $11,743.70 at Boss Selection Singapore on 29 March 2010.

In court, Sean Colville Niven stated that the $11,743.70 charge at Boss Selection Singapore was for clothing for himself and Wang Zijian and that he considered such clothing to be part of his employment package.

APG paid legal fees to HW Litigation and Butcher Paul & Calder in relation to Suit No 170 of 2009, a suit brought by JL Property Group Pte Ltd against, among others, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian, although APG was not a party to that suit.
Sean Colville Niven received settlement money from JL Property Group Pte Ltd in relation to Suit No 170 of 2009 and did not pay any part of that settlement money to APG.

Sean Colville Niven stated that there had been a discussion and agreement with shareholders and non-executive directors that any money he recovered from Suit No 170 of 2009 did not need to be paid to APG.

APG made payments directly to Sean Colville Niven described in its records as loans, commission, furniture, artwork, and payments to his IRAS account or loan account, without supporting documentation showing the underlying transactions.
APG paid for air travel for Sean Colville Niven, his wife, his children and the family's domestic helper, including a trip to France by Sean Colville Niven and his wife in August 2009.
APG paid telephone charges for Sean Colville Niven and his children, his children's school fees, and rental and leasing of furniture.

In February 2010, Sean Colville Niven signed an APG invoice to Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd for A$86,000 in marketing funds and wrote instructions on the invoice for the funds to be paid into his personal bank account in Australia.
Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd paid A$86,000 into Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account following the 22 February 2010 invoice.
In court, Sean Colville Niven stated that Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd preferred to pay him personally and that he considered himself and APG to be effectively the same for that purpose.

Sean Colville Niven stated that the A$86,000 from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd was spent exclusively on the marketing campaign and that his assistant, Vanessa Foo, collected receipts and passed them to APG's Accounts Department.
Sean Colville Niven did not call his assistant, Vanessa Foo, to give evidence about the handling of receipts for the A$86,000 marketing funds.

In November 2009 and December 2009, Sean Colville Niven drew three OCBC cheques: cheque No 771893 dated 17 November 2009 for $320,000 payable to Ms Lim, cheque No 771397 dated 17 November 2009 for $300,000 payable to APG, and cheque No 771399 dated 1 December 2009 for $490,000 payable to APG.

Part of the proceeds of the Ubertas A$900,000 loan was deposited into Sean Colville Niven's personal account in Australia and then transmitted to APG via the Singapore dollar cheques he drew in favour of APG and Ms Lim.
In evidence, Sean Colville Niven stated that he had intended to use the Ubertas loan funds as paid-up capital for APG but later received legal advice that this could not be done.

In his email of 18 July 2010, Sean Colville Niven wrote that APG had been under financial pressure every month since repaying the loan to Goh Cheng Liang and committing to monthly repayments of A$40,000 to Ubertas.
In February 2009, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian opened a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx.
Between 6 February 2009 and 31 May 2009, funds were regularly transferred from APG's bank account into the joint UOB account held by Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian.

On 8 December 2009, after the non-executive directors had resigned, APG's board, consisting of Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Ms Lim, passed a resolution allowing cheques drawn on APG's bank account to be signed by a single signatory, either Sean Colville Niven or Ms Lim.
After the 8 December 2009 resolution, Sean Colville Niven held APG's cheque books, and his personal assistant, Vanessa Foo, had access to the cheque books, bank tokens and bank balances.

In his defence, Sean Colville Niven stated that APG's funds used for his personal benefit were authorised by APG's directors and shareholders as part of his employment package and that there was an oral agreement and a practice for such payments.
Sean Colville Niven stated that, to all intents and purposes, APG was regarded as his business and that it was up to him to run APG as he deemed fit.

Sean Colville Niven counterclaimed against APG for unpaid monthly salary of $75,000 for five months from October 2010 to February 2011, mobile phone charges, and his share of Back End Commission of $106,944 or A$83,550, and sought an account of Back End Commission payments under the September Agreement.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, prior to the commencement of the dispute, payments made by APG to or for the benefit of Sean Colville Niven, and the recorded unpaid share capital associated with his APG shareholdings, remained enforceable obligations in light of his roles, the September and October 2010 agreements, APG's financial condition, and the flow of funds between APG, his personal accounts and the joint account with Wang Zijian.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors and shareholders of a company may be required to account for company funds they receive or that are applied for their benefit, particularly where payments lack supporting documentation or relate to personal expenses. Shareholders recorded as holding issued shares can be to the company for unpaid share capital, with liability typically attaching to the registered holder at the time a call is made. Agreements between a company and its directors that adjust or release such obligations may be subject to scrutiny, especially where the company is insolvent or near insolvency and the interests of creditors must be considered.
Party_Details.Application: As an executive director responsible for APG's finances, Sean Colville Niven was involved in decisions about payments and banking arrangements, including the creation of a joint account with Wang Zijian and the change to single-signatory cheque authority. APG's records show substantial payments connected to him, including credit card bills, legal fees for litigation in which APG was not a party, travel and family-related expenses, and payments without supporting documentation, as well as directing marketing funds from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd to his personal account. The September Agreement recorded a significant debt from him to APG and allocated unpaid share capital to him, while the October Agreement recorded his resignation and transfer of shares and stated that he would owe nothing to APG upon settlement except for tax. During this period, APG faced cash flow difficulties, creditor pressure and loan obligations. These circumstances formed the basis for later legal analysis of whether the payments and share capital obligations remained payable by him or had been effectively released or restructured.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the use of APG's funds for Sean Colville Niven's personal expenses created a prima facie case of misuse of company funds and that he bore the burden of justifying the payments. It found that many payments, including American Express bills, legal fees for Suit No 170 of 2009, travel and family expenses, and payments without supporting documents, were not shown to be for APG's benefit. The court rejected his assertions of an Account Practice and Oral Agreement as unsubstantiated and inconsistent with the loan agreement with Goh Cheng Liang and the conduct of the non-executive directors. It concluded that the September and October Agreements were entered into when APG was insolvent or near insolvency and that they improperly reduced or postponed his liabilities to APG and shifted some of his personal debts to APG, contrary to creditors' interests. Applying insolvency-related fiduciary principles, the court set aside the Agreements as voidable and held that they did not relieve him of liability. On unpaid share capital, the court determined that he remained liable for $400,000 corresponding to shares he still held when APG made a call, and that he was not liable for capital on the 240,000 shares transferred to Ms Lim, as liability followed the registered holder. The court ordered him to pay $400,000 for unpaid share capital, to repay $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10 (less amounts recovered from the first defendant) as moneys paid for his personal use, and, jointly with Wang Zijian, to account for and, if not justified, repay sums paid by APG into their joint UOB account. His counterclaim, based on the September Agreement, was dismissed because that agreement was held invalid.

Party_Details.Name: Wang Zijian (James Wang)

Facts.Text: Wang Zijian, also known as James Wang, worked with Sean Colville Niven at JL Property Group Pte Ltd before December 2008 in the business of selling Australian properties.
In December 2008, Wang Zijian and Sean Colville Niven decided to start their own company to sell Australian properties and resigned from JL Property Group Pte Ltd.
In December 2008, Wang Zijian and Sean Colville Niven approached Wang's godfather, Goh Cheng Liang, for a $900,000 loan as start-up capital for their new venture.
On incorporation of APG, Wang Zijian was a director and shareholder holding 160,000 ordinary shares, which were issued as fully paid-up although the share capital was not actually paid.
After Ms Lim joined APG on 15 May 2009, Wang Zijian was APG's sales manager.

Non-executive directors Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren sat on APG's board to protect the interests of lender Goh Cheng Liang, while Wang Zijian and Sean Colville Niven controlled day-to-day operations.

On 6 September 2010, Wang Zijian signed the September Agreement with APG, Sean Colville Niven and Ms Lim, which recorded that he was for $160,000 of unpaid share capital and that unpaid capital would be repaid at 10% per year over ten years.

On 13 October 2010, Wang Zijian signed the handwritten October Agreement with Sean Colville Niven and Ms Lim, which recorded that he would resign as a director that day and transfer all his shares in APG to Ms Lim except for 10% retained in his hands.
The October Agreement recorded that upon settlement, Wang Zijian would have no liability to APG from a compliance perspective and would owe nothing to APG except for personal tax liabilities.

In his defence, Wang Zijian did not dispute that APG's funds were used for Sean Colville Niven's personal benefit but stated that such use was authorised by APG's directors and shareholders as part of an employment package and practice.
Wang Zijian and Sean Colville Niven opened a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx, in February 2009.
Between 6 February 2009 and 31 May 2009, funds were regularly transferred from APG's bank account into the joint UOB account held by Wang Zijian and Sean Colville Niven.
Funds transferred from APG into the joint UOB account could be used by Wang Zijian and Sean Colville Niven without further reference to APG's non-executive directors.

On 8 December 2009, after the non-executive directors had resigned, APG's board, consisting of Wang Zijian, Sean Colville Niven and Ms Lim, passed a resolution allowing cheques drawn on APG's bank account to be signed by a single signatory, either Sean Colville Niven or Ms Lim.

In his defence, Wang Zijian stated that cheque payments from APG's incorporation until 7 November 2009 were authorised or endorsed by himself, Sean Colville Niven and at least one non-executive director, and that cheque payments from 8 December 2009 onwards were signed and authorised by at least two of the three directors.

Wang Zijian stated that payments for Sean Colville Niven's personal expenses were permitted and approved by APG's directors and shareholders as part of an employment package and that there was a practice for approval and authorisation of payments and use of APG's funds.

Wang Zijian counterclaimed against APG for unpaid monthly salary of $25,000 for five months from October 2010 to February 2011, reimbursement of $3,000 mobile phone charges, his share of Back End Commission of $53,472 or A$41,775, and an account of Back End Commission payments under the September Agreement.

In reply submissions, Wang Zijian stated that he had not personally incurred or spent any of the expenses to be unauthorised and that from May 2010 he had been in Malaysia and no longer involved in APG's daily operations or management.
Wang Zijian admitted in court that he had never paid for the 160,000 APG shares issued to him on incorporation.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and financial arrangements, Wang Zijian remained responsible for unpaid share capital on his APG shares and for accounting for APG funds transferred into the joint UOB account with Sean Colville Niven, in circumstances where he participated in APG's management, signed the September and October 2010 agreements, and was a joint holder of the account receiving APG funds.
Party_Details.Rule: A shareholder recorded as holding issued shares may be to the company for unpaid share capital, with liability typically attaching to the registered holder at the time a call is made. Directors and joint account holders who receive company funds into a joint account can be required to account for those funds and to show that they were applied for company purposes. Agreements between a company and its directors that purport to release or restructure such obligations may be subject to challenge, particularly where the company is insolvent or near insolvency and creditor interests are engaged.
Party_Details.Application: Wang Zijian held 160,000 APG shares issued as fully paid-up but for which he acknowledged he had not paid. He was a director and sales manager and, together with Sean Colville Niven, opened a joint UOB account that received regular transfers from APG's bank account in early 2009. After the non-executive directors resigned, he participated in a board resolution that enabled single-signatory cheques, increasing the ability of executive management to move APG funds. The September Agreement recorded his unpaid share capital liability and a long-term repayment schedule, and the October Agreement recorded his resignation, transfer of most of his shares to Ms Lim, and a statement that he would owe nothing to APG upon settlement except for tax. APG was experiencing cash flow difficulties and creditor pressure during this period. These facts formed the basis for later legal analysis of whether he remained for unpaid share capital and for accounting for APG funds transferred into the joint account, notwithstanding the September and October Agreements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Wang Zijian admitted he had not paid for his 160,000 APG shares and held that, as the registered holder at the time APG made a call on unpaid capital, he was liable to pay $160,000, subject to the court's analysis of the September and October Agreements. It interpreted APG's articles and general company law to conclude that liability for unpaid capital follows the registered holder at the time of the call and does not remain with a former holder after a transfer, so his liability was limited to his own 160,000 shares. The court held that the September and October Agreements, which purported to restructure and postpone unpaid capital obligations and to release the defendants from liabilities, were voidable because they were entered into when APG was insolvent or near insolvency and were not in the interests of APG's creditors. Accordingly, those Agreements did not relieve him of liability. On the flow of funds, the court noted that APG transferred money into the joint UOB account held by him and Sean Colville Niven and that this arrangement allowed them to bypass cheque-signing controls. It held that both defendants were jointly and severally obliged to account for all sums paid by APG into the joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes. The court accepted that Wang Zijian did not personally receive the specific personal expenses paid for Sean Colville Niven but held that, as a joint account holder and director, he was still responsible to account for APG funds that entered the joint account. His counterclaim, based on the September Agreement, was dismissed because that agreement was held invalid.
